title: Shell gold gilding
materials: shell gold (powdered gold bound in gum arabic, in a pan)
gum-ammoniac solution (5g gum ammoniac crystals dissolved in water)
soft brush (size 0 or 000) for applying gold
glassine or crystal parchment for burnishing through
sources: Shell gold technique documented in Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain; chapter on illumination and gilding. Medieval gilding tradition in manuscript illumination; documented also in Cennino Cennini, Il Libro dell'Arte (c.1400) — public domain translation by Daniel V. Thompson (1933).
notes: Shell gold is finely ground gold powder bound in gum arabic, painted on like watercolour and then burnished to a metallic finish. Distinct from loose-leaf gold (which is applied to raised gesso under size). Shell gold is the simpler technique for small lettering, hair-lines, and details. Covers: reactivating dry shell gold (breathing on it, adding a drop of water), painting it on with a fine brush in two thin layers rather than one thick, allowing each coat to dry completely, burnishing cold through glassine with the agate burnisher. Common failure mode: burnishing before fully dry (gold tears). When to use gum ammoniac size instead: when a raised surface is wanted.
